reasoning: All three correctly reframe downtown as a residency/dwell-time problem rather than retail, but B is the most rigorous: it explicitly names the substitution bias ('what easier question my mind wants to answer'), uses Meadows' leverage hierarchy to explain WHY the failed fixes were predictable rather than just asserting it, attaches an explicit ~80% confidence to its diagnosis while flagging lower certainty on remedies, and red-teams its own vacancy-tax option (landlords passing cost to weak tenants). C is close behind: tight assumptions (population range, pre-1960 stock), the sharp 'measurement artifact' reframe, and the cleanest actionable verdict (run A+B in parallel, C as generator, scale on data), though it red-teams its own options less. A is solid and concrete with useful trip-count estimates, but those numbers (80 workers to 200-400 trips; 1,000 members to $240k) are asserted without sourcing or sensitivity, it surfaces fewer cognitive traps, and its 'uncertainty' notes are thinner than B's and C's calibrated confidence statements.
